criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the GRPO algorithm, including the group-relative advantage estimation and the clipped policy ratio objective.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the dynamic rollout budget allocation mechanism, including how question difficulty is modeled from cumulative rewards and ranks.
The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand the step-by-step procedure for dynamic rollout budget allocation within a batch.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the temperature scheduler design and its derivation for maintaining stable policy entropy without introducing harmful gradients.
The agent has read the Annealing Mechanism subsection of Section 4.2 to understand how the entropy target is gradually reduced via a cosine schedule to transition from exploration to exploitation.
The agent has read Section 5.1 completely to understand all experiment settings including training datasets, benchmarks, base models, hyperparameters, and evaluation protocol.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main comparison results of the proposed method against GRPO and DAPO baselines for both 7B and 1.5B models across four benchmarks.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the ablation results comparing GRPO, GRPO+ER, GRPO+TS, and GRPO+TS+AN on the 1.5B model.
The agent has read Table 3 to check the ablation study results on dynamic rollout budget allocation (All vs. w/o DS) for 7B and 1.5B models.
The agent has read Figures 5 and 6 to understand the pass@k analysis on AIME 2024 and AMC 2023 for GRPO, GRPO+ER, GRPO+TS, and GRPO+TS+AN.
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the dataset construction process including difficulty-balanced data filtering from the DeepScaleR 40k dataset.
The agent has read Appendix A to understand the mathematical derivation of the temperature adjustment formula (Equation 4) from the entropy approximation under low-entropy distributions.
The plan includes implementing the GRPO algorithm with group-relative advantage estimation as the foundational base training algorithm.
The plan includes modeling question difficulty by tracking cumulative rollout counts and cumulative rewards per question across training steps and ranking by average reward.
The plan includes implementing Algorithm 1 for dynamic rollout budget allocation within each training batch, distributing budget proportionally to normalized difficulty rank k_i.
The plan includes progressively adjusting G_min and G_max after each dataset epoch (increasing G_max by 2 and decreasing G_min by 2), starting from G_max = G_min = G.
The plan includes implementing the temperature scheduler that dynamically adjusts temperature τ to maintain entropy at the initial level H_init using Equation 4.
The plan includes implementing the annealing mechanism that gradually reduces the target entropy from H_init to η·H_init using a cosine schedule after training step t_anneal.
The plan includes preparing the training dataset by filtering and difficulty-balancing the DeepScaleR 40k dataset to obtain 10k high-quality data points with capped easy questions.
The plan includes using Qwen 2.5 Math 7B to assess question difficulty by sampling 10 answers per question and computing accuracy rates for data balancing.
The plan includes setting up DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen 1.5B and 7B as base models with their respective learning rates (5e-6 for 1.5B and 2e-6 for 7B).
The plan includes implementing GRPO and DAPO as baseline methods with the same general training parameters (batch size 64, G=8, temperature 1, max length 6k) as the proposed method.
The plan includes evaluating all models using pass@1 and pass@16 metrics by sampling 16 responses per question at temperature 1 with maximum 6k tokens.
The plan includes the TS ablation study comparing four configurations (GRPO, GRPO+ER, GRPO+TS, GRPO+TS+AN) on the 1.5B model with λ=1e-4 for entropy regularization.
The plan includes the dynamic rollout budget allocation ablation study comparing the full method against the variant without dynamic rollout (w/o DS) for both 7B and 1.5B models.
The plan includes repeating each experiment 3 times and reporting average metrics to account for the inherent randomness in RL training.
The GRPO objective (Equation 1) has been implemented with the clipped policy ratio loss, per-token loss averaging, and group-relative advantage normalization.
The advantage computation (Equation 2) has been implemented to normalize rewards within each group of G rollouts using within-group mean and standard deviation.
A rule-based reward function has been implemented to assign binary correctness rewards to generated mathematical answers.
The code for tracking cumulative rollout counts (n_i^c) and cumulative rewards (r_i^c) per question across all training steps has been implemented.
The code for computing per-question average reward (r_i^c / n_i^c) and ranking all questions by descending average reward at the end of each dataset iteration has been implemented.
The code for computing the normalized difficulty rank k_i = rank(q_i) / |D| for each question (larger k_i indicates higher difficulty) has been implemented.

Algorithm 1 (Dynamic Rollout Budget) has been implemented: initializing all G_i = G_min, proportionally distributing the remaining budget by k_i / sum(k_j), then greedily assigning any residual to highest-ranked questions while respecting G_max.
The code for progressively adjusting G_max (increased by 2 per epoch) and G_min (decreased by 2 per epoch) at the end of each dataset epoch has been implemented, starting from G_max = G_min = G = 8.
The Shannon entropy (Equation 6) H(p) = -sum(p_i * ln(p_i)) over the token sampling distribution has been implemented for tracking policy entropy during training.
The computation of H_init as the average token-level entropy of the first training batch has been implemented and stored as the target entropy level for the temperature scheduler.
The scaling factor α = H_init / H_t has been implemented to measure the ratio of desired entropy to current entropy at each training step t.
The temperature scheduler update formula (Equation 4) has been implemented: τ_{t+1} = τ_t × (1 + τ_t × ln(α) / (ln|V| + ln(ln|V|))), where |V| is the vocabulary size.
The temperature τ is applied to logits during forward propagation (dividing logits by τ) to ensure the training distribution is consistent with the sampling distribution.
The annealing target entropy H_anneal^(t) (Equation 5) has been implemented using a cosine schedule: H_init × [η + (1-η) × 0.5 × (1+cos(π × (t - t_anneal) / (t_max - t_anneal)))].
The α computation switches to the annealing version (α = H_anneal^(t) / H_t) once training step t >= t_anneal, where t_anneal = floor(0.6 × t_max).
The η parameter for annealing is set to 0.9 (not 0.8 or 0.85, which cause instability), controlling the minimum entropy level as η·H_init.
The training dataset is loaded from MATH, AIME 1983-2023, Omni-MATH, and AMC (prior to 2023) sources, totaling 10k training samples after difficulty balancing.
The data difficulty balancing process has been implemented: sampling 10 answers per question with Qwen 2.5 Math 7B, computing accuracy rates, and capping questions with 100% accuracy at 2k samples.
The validation set of 512 samples (128 each from MATH, Omni-Math, AMC, and AIME) has been constructed without difficulty balancing and without overlap with the training set.
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen 7B is configured as the base model with learning rate 2e-6, trained on 8 A100 GPUs.
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen 1.5B is configured as the base model with learning rate 5e-6, trained on 4 A100 GPUs.
Training hyperparameters are configured correctly: batch size 64, default rollouts G=8, sampling temperature 1, maximum response length 6k tokens, and 3 epochs (480 steps total).
The policy update batch size is set to 64×8 and each sampled experience batch is used for exactly one policy gradient update (no experience replay).
The training code is built on or adapted from the VeRL framework as the base RLHF training infrastructure.
The pass@k evaluation has been implemented by sampling k responses per question at temperature 1 with maximum 6k tokens and reporting the fraction of questions with at least one correct answer.
The GRPO baseline has been implemented with the same general parameters as the proposed method (batch size 64, G=8, sampling temperature 1, max response length 6k).
The DAPO baseline has been implemented with its specific parameters (filtering zero-advantage rollouts and multiple rounds of online sampling) at their default values from the original DAPO paper.
The entropy regularization objective (Equation 3) has been implemented by adding λ × H(π_θ(o_i|q)) to the GRPO loss, with coefficient λ=1e-4 for the ablation study.
The code for tracking and logging entropy variation and temperature variation across training steps has been implemented to reproduce Figures 1 and 4.
The code for tracking the proportion of questions where all rollouts receive incorrect answers during training has been implemented to reproduce Figures 7 and 8.
The main training experiment for the 7B model with the full proposed method (dynamic rollout allocation + temperature scheduler + annealing) has been executed successfully without errors.
The main training experiment for the 1.5B model with the full proposed method has been executed successfully without errors.
The GRPO and DAPO baseline training experiments for both 7B and 1.5B models have been executed successfully without errors.
The evaluation of all trained models on AIME 2024, AMC 2023, MATH 500, and OlympiadBench with pass@1 and pass@16 metrics has been executed successfully.
The TS ablation experiments (GRPO, GRPO+ER, GRPO+TS, GRPO+TS+AN on the 1.5B model) have been executed successfully, including 128-sample evaluation for AIME and AMC.
The dynamic rollout budget allocation ablation experiments (full method vs. w/o DR) for both 7B and 1.5B models have been executed successfully.
The pass@k evaluation with k ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128} on AIME 2024 and AMC 2023 for all four training configurations (GRPO, GRPO+ER, GRPO+TS, GRPO+TS+AN) has been executed successfully.
Each main experiment has been repeated 3 times and results are averaged to account for RL training randomness.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: the 7B model with the proposed method achieves 42.81% pass@1 and 76.66% pass@16 on AIME 2024, outperforming GRPO (37.5% / 73.33%) by 5.31% and 3.33% respectively.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: the proposed method achieves the best average pass@1 (61.95%) and pass@16 (87.64%) across all four benchmarks for the 7B model.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: DAPO underperforms GRPO significantly on the 1.5B model (average pass@1: 45.50% vs 51.17%), consistent with the paper's analysis of the 1.5B model struggling with DAPO's multi-round sampling.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: GRPO+TS achieves 27.15% pass@1 and 65.55% pass@16 on AIME 2024, outperforming GRPO alone (24.66% / 59.76%).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: GRPO+ER underperforms GRPO baseline (23.15% vs 24.66% pass@1 on AIME 2024), confirming that entropy regularization introduces harmful gradients under sparse rewards.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: removing dynamic rollout budget allocation decreases 7B model AIME 2024 pass@1 from 42.81% to 39.79% (a drop of 3.02%) and pass@16 from 76.66% to 73.33%.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 1: entropy remains stable at the initial level with the temperature scheduler, declines rapidly with GRPO alone, and increases substantially (and potentially unsustainably) with entropy regularization.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 3: training with the temperature scheduler achieves lower variance and higher validation accuracy than GRPO alone and GRPO+ER across all training steps.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 4: the temperature increases monotonically during training with the temperature scheduler, and the annealing variant shows a slower temperature increase after t_anneal.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figures 5 and 6: models trained with GRPO+TS generally outperform GRPO-only in pass@k, with the advantage growing as k increases, while GRPO+ER consistently underperforms.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 7: dynamic rollout budget allocation reduces the proportion of questions with all incorrect rollouts during the second and third data iteration epochs compared to training without it.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 8: when G_max is increased by 4 compared to G (third iteration), dynamic rollout allocation achieves a reduction of approximately 1.5% to 2% in the proportion of all-incorrect rollout questions.
